architecture.summary: This is a systematic literature review of retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems. It does not present a novel system but instead surveys and synthesises empirical evidence across 128 studies (January 2020–May 2025) examining how RAG—which grounds LLM outputs in retrieved evidence to reduce hallucinations and outdated knowledge—compares to parametric-only baselines. The review maps datasets, architectures, evaluation practices, and identifies research gaps. Methods have evolved from DPR + seq2seq baselines to modular, policy-driven RAG with hybrid/structure-aware retrieval, uncertainty-triggered loops, memory, and multimodality.

biblio.title: A Systematic Literature Review of Retrieval-Augmented Generation: Techniques, Metrics, and Challenges

claims.statement: We included 128 studies spanning knowledge-intensive tasks (35/128; 27.3%), open-domain QA (20/128; 15.6%), software engineering (13/128; 10.2%), and medical domains (11/128; 8.6%).
Methods have shifted from DPR + seq2seq baselines to modular, policy-driven RAG with hybrid/structure-aware retrieval, uncertainty-triggered loops, memory, and emerging multimodality.
Evaluation remains overlap-heavy (EM/F1), with increasing use of retrieval diagnostics (e.g., Recall@k, MRR@k), human judgements, and LLM-as-judge protocols.
Efficiency and security (poisoning, leakage, jailbreaks) are growing concerns.
Evidence supports a shift to modular, policy-driven RAG, combining hybrid/structure-aware retrieval, uncertainty-aware control, memory, and multimodality, to improve grounding and efficiency.

To advance from prototypes to dependable systems, we recommend: (i) holistic benchmarks pairing quality with cost/latency and safety, (ii) budget-aware retrieval/tool-use policies, and (iii) provenance-aware pipelines that expose uncertainty and deliver traceable evidence.
No meta-analysis was undertaken due to heterogeneity of designs and metrics.
The evidence base may be affected by citation-lag from the inclusion thresholds and by English-only, five-library coverage.
